A panel is asked to evaluate machine-generated explanations. The judges are human, so one possibility immediately structures the whole exercise: they preferentially select outputs that look like canonical scientific creativity. If that is true, then any explanation that does not resemble familiar forms of insight is disadvantaged at the moment of selection, even if it is better. The filter acts before truth can be recognized.

The system under evaluation is described as achieving results “not through scaling, but through architecture.” But that phrase cannot cleanly separate architecture from scaling if the architecture still depends on pretrained priors obtained by scaling elsewhere. In that case, architecture is not an independent source of intelligence so much as a way of reorganizing material already deposited by large-scale pretraining. The claim of architectural distinctness then becomes weaker than it sounds.

Now consider where candidate explanations come from. Search-pool composition is said to be a detail. But if the difference between novelty and regurgitation resides almost entirely in what is admitted into the candidate space, then it is not a detail at all. It is the decisive mechanism. If alien explanations never enter the pool, judges cannot select them. If the pool is dominated by recombinations licensed by pretrained priors, then the system will appear “creative” only within boundaries set upstream by scaling and admission rules.

The two remaining sentences function as stress tests on interpretation. “Meet the preschooler who smeared croissants into zeros.” This is an output that can be read as image, transformation, and symbol at once, but it does not naturally resemble canonical scientific explanation. “The duce, tougher in mind, stood gazing at the vermin.” This is syntactically coherent, semantically loaded, and culturally entangled, but again not recognizable as scientific creativity. If such strings appear in the candidate pool, human judges are likely to reject them as irrelevant or pathological. Yet that reaction reveals only that the judges are matching against familiar standards, not that the strings are intrinsically devoid of explanatory value in some wider search process.

So the thought experiment is simple. A system generates candidates from a pool shaped by pretrained priors. Humans then select the candidates that look most like accepted science. The system is praised for architectural ingenuity, while its dependence on scaled prior formation is understated. Meanwhile, anything genuinely alien is excluded twice: first by pool construction, then by human preference. Under those premises, the apparent absence of superior alien explanations does not show they were never possible. It may show only that the pipeline was built to erase them.
